title: Sonnet 32 - The first time that the sun rose on thine oath by Elizabeth Barrett Browning — Love Poetry Lines
content:
The first time that the sun rose on thine oath
To love me, I looked forward to the moon
To slacken all those bonds which seemed too soon
And quickly tied to make a lasting troth.
Quick-loving hearts, I thought, may quickly loathe;
And, looking on myself, I seemed not one
For such man's love!—more like an out-of-tune
Worn viol, a good singer would be wroth
meaning: "Sonnet 32 - The first time that the sun rose on thine oath" by Elizabeth Barrett Browning is a love and deep and nature poem consisting of 14 lines. The poet explores themes of love and deep and nature through vivid imagery and emotional depth. Beginning with "The first time that the sun rose on thine oath To love me, I looked forward to the moon...", this piece captures the essence of human experience and invites reflection on the deeper currents of life and feeling. This work is part of Elizabeth Barrett Browning's celebrated body of poetry that continues to resonate with readers worldwide.
